non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Many non-safety units also face hiring strain; must compare intensity and qualification bottlenecks
expected_textual_signals: vacancies; hard to recruit; applicant shortage; retention bonus; signing bonus; cannot fill academy; lateral competition
likely_source_types: BLS; PERF; city HR reports; budgets; news
possible_quant_data: applicants per opening; vacancy duration; hires; separations; academy fill rates
confounds: processing delays; standards changes; fiscal hiring freezes

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Not a general municipal explanation and should not be overgeneralized from police to fire or non-safety; distinct from COVID-era hiring disruption, pension-vintage retirement timing, and the broad 2021-2023 Great Resignation, which are separate channels needing separate evidence and would also show up in non-safety municipal hiring
expected_textual_signals: morale; scrutiny; public criticism; George Floyd; protests; resignations; retirements; unsupported by city leaders -- text must name the specific channel (legitimacy/scrutiny vs. COVID hiring disruption vs. retirement-eligibility timing vs. general labor-market competition) rather than being scored from any post-2020 staffing mention
likely_source_types: PERF; news; union statements; city staffing reports
possible_quant_data: applications over time; retirements; resignations; survey responses; compare police quit-rate change to overall municipal/private-sector quit-rate change over the same window
confounds: COVID labor disruption; crime trends; agency competition; pension-vintage retirement bulges unrelated to any 2020 shock

hypothesis_name: compensating differentials for risk stress and hours
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Transit, sanitation, nursing, corrections, and public works emergencies may also carry high burden
expected_textual_signals: dangerous; stressful; injury; trauma; shift work; 24/7; mandatory overtime; on call
likely_source_types: BLS; NIOSH; local reports; arbitration awards
possible_quant_data: injury rates; OT hours; disability retirements; schedule premiums
confounds: occupational culture may exaggerate or understate burden in text

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Other coverage-constrained services may also spiral when vacancies rise
expected_textual_signals: mandatory overtime; callback; minimum staffing; backfill; exhausted workforce; burnout
possible_quant_data: overtime spending; hours; vacancies; sick leave; resignation hazards
confounds: overtime may be effect rather than cause

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Teacher and other public bargaining may also use peers; not public-safety-specific by default; Massachusetts teacher bargaining commonly invokes 'surrounding' or 'comparable' district salary comparisons, but without an interest-arbitration backstop to enforce the comparison the way JLMC can for police/fire, the comparator claim may function more as aspirational or budget-override-campaign rhetoric than as a binding standard (see teacher_peer_district_comparability discussion in non_safety_teacher_wage_mechanism_refinement_2026-07-04.md); CONFIRMED 2026-07-04 (see ma_teacher_bargaining_school_finance_institutional_context_2026-07-04.md): JLMC comparator criteria are statutorily mandated for arbitrators to weigh, while teacher-side comparator claims have no equivalent compulsory enforcement mechanism
expected_textual_signals: comparable communities; parity; surrounding cities; peer departments; wage survey
likely_source_types: awards; bargaining packets; meeting materials; some CBAs
possible_quant_data: peer wage trends; matched text scores; downstream wage changes

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Some non-safety sectors also use mediation/factfinding, so bare backstop presence is not unique; VERIFIED 2026-07-04 for Massachusetts (see ma_teacher_bargaining_school_finance_institutional_context_2026-07-04.md): the sharper, confirmed comparison is that JLMC orders police/fire into compulsory binding interest arbitration upon certified impasse, while teachers and most other MA public employees under M.G.L. c. 150E Section 9 have only mediation plus advisory (non-binding) factfinding, after which the employer may unilaterally implement its last, best offer; a voluntary arbitration route exists on paper for non-police/fire units but requires mutual agreement plus employer/school-committee authorization and is not typical in practice (Mass.gov's own guidance describes non-police/fire bargaining as having 'no arbitration process') -- this is a compulsory-vs-voluntary institutional-design difference, not a has-backstop-vs-has-no-process-at-all difference
expected_textual_signals: interest arbitration; impasse; factfinding; JLMC; stipulated award; mediation; explicit no-strike prohibition paired with the impasse process; union and employer final positions plus awarded figure (for later bunching/centering analysis, not for collection-time classification); for non-safety MA units, look for 'advisory' or 'recommendations' language describing factfinding, or 'last best offer' unilateral-implementation language, as the textual signature of the weaker process
likely_source_types: awards; JLMC materials; legal sources; bargaining summaries; MA DLR impasse/factfinding materials for non-safety comparison
possible_quant_data: award incidence; settlement timing; wage outcomes with/without backstop; award-to-offer-gap ratio for splitting vs. criteria-applying test; conventional vs. final-offer-arbitration jurisdictions as a comparison; frequency of unilateral last-best-offer implementation in non-safety MA bargaining as the analog outcome measure
confounds: visibility in documents may exceed causal importance; MA/JLMC is one institutional case, not the national arbitration story

hypothesis_name: political salience and public safety premium
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Schools, transit, sanitation, snow removal, and public health can also be highly salient
expected_textual_signals: public safety; cannot leave positions unfilled; residents demand coverage; elected pressure
likely_source_types: budgets; council minutes; news; arbitration findings
possible_quant_data: budget reallocations; emergency appropriations; staffing mandates
confounds: salience is hard to measure and may vary by event

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Could overlap with arbitration rules and legal strike constraints rather than independent power
expected_textual_signals: protected bargaining status; statutory process; leverage; no-strike substitution
likely_source_types: state statutes; arbitration scholarship; awards; labor law references
possible_quant_data: contract cycle length; impasse frequency; settlement patterns
confounds: difficult to separate from fiscal capacity and labor scarcity

hypothesis_name: budget capacity and property tax base
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: General explanation for many municipal wages, not just safety; VERIFIED 2026-07-04 for Massachusetts school finance (see ma_teacher_bargaining_school_finance_institutional_context_2026-07-04.md): schools face a two-sided constraint most other municipal departments (including police/fire) do not -- a state-mandated spending floor (net school spending = Chapter 70 aid plus required local contribution, per DESE's foundation-budget formula, with no municipality required to fund more than 82.5% of its foundation budget locally) plus the same municipal Proposition 2 1/2 levy-limit ceiling shared by all departments; the floor is school-specific, the ceiling is general, and override votes are the mechanism for raising the ceiling
expected_textual_signals: ability to pay; tax base; budget reserves; levy limit; revenue growth; override; state aid; net school spending; foundation budget; required local contribution
likely_source_types: budgets; CAFRs; census; awards; DESE Chapter 70 program pages and district net-school-spending reports
possible_quant_data: assessed value; tax base; own-source revenue; debt; district-level net school spending vs. foundation budget compliance over time
confounds: capacity may raise all wages and service spending; the state-aid floor could mask or offset local fiscal-capacity variation in ways ordinary municipal ability-to-pay measures do not capture

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Many municipal jobs also require licensing or CDL-style credentials
expected_textual_signals: academy; EMT; certification; continuing education; physical test; background check
likely_source_types: BLS; NVFC; academy standards; local hiring materials
possible_quant_data: pass rates; training duration; credential maintenance cost
confounds: requirements may proxy quality standards more than scarcity

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Mostly fire-specific; non-safety comparison comes through service model rather than direct wage setting; transmission is lagged and indirect through three distinct channels -- (1) combination-department conversion/new paid-on-call stipend lines, (2) overtime/minimum-staffing substitution in existing career units, (3) full volunteer-to-career professionalization creating a brand-new bargaining unit -- rather than direct growth in an existing career CBA's base wage
expected_textual_signals: volunteer shortage; paid on call; combination department; daytime response gap -- text should specify which of the three transmission channels is being described so it is coded as volunteer-pipeline evidence vs. career wage-setting evidence per the two-corpus discipline
likely_source_types: NVFC; NFPA/FPRF; local fire studies; news
possible_quant_data: volunteer headcount; turnout; paid staffing growth; stipend adoption; sequence of volunteer-decline evidence relative to new paid/combination staffing lines or new bargaining units
confounds: may affect budgets or staffing mix more than base wages; effect may show up years before (or instead of) negotiated base-wage change

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Other municipal jobs can also face liability, especially corrections, nursing, transit, and utilities
expected_textual_signals: liability; lawsuit risk; line-of-duty injury; PTSD; disability retirement
possible_quant_data: workers comp; disability pensions; claims costs; leave usage
confounds: may overlap with hazard/stress rather than separate channel

non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Teachers are public-facing, credentialed, unionized, and generally well-regarded, yet NCES School Pulse Panel data show 74% of public schools had difficulty filling at least one teaching vacancy entering 2024-25 (down from 79%), with lack of qualified candidates (64%) and too few applicants (62%) as the top two challenges, and special education/bilingual-ESL vacancies hardest to fill by grade band; public esteem for teachers should not be read as evidence of abundant supply at the credentialed, subject-qualified margin, the same caution already applied to firefighters
expected_textual_signals: teacher shortage; vacancy; long-term substitute; emergency certification; provisional license; under-certified; hard-to-fill; lack of qualified candidates; too few applicants
likely_source_types: NCES/IES School Pulse Panel; Learning Policy Institute; state education agency staffing reports; district HR reports
possible_quant_data: vacancy rate; applicants per opening; share of positions filled by fully certified teachers; long-term substitute usage
confounds: shortage is concentrated by subject/grade/geography rather than uniform, so aggregate 'teacher shortage' language can overstate a district's actual exposure

hypothesis_name: teacher shortage buffering without wage adjustment
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Teacher staffing strain is often absorbed through long-term substitutes, emergency/provisional-certified hires, larger class sizes, or reduced program offerings rather than through base-wage increases, unlike police/fire overtime and callback which raise realized compensation paid to existing incumbent staff even without a base-wage change; substitute pay is typically well below a certified teacher's wage, so this buffering channel need not show up as increased personnel spending at all
expected_textual_signals: long-term substitute; emergency certification; provisional license; under-certified; class size increase; unfilled position; program reduction; combined caseload
likely_source_types: district staffing reports; school committee minutes; state education agency data; news
possible_quant_data: substitute usage/spending; class-size trends; share of positions filled by non-fully-certified teachers; program/course offering changes
confounds: some CBAs include class-size-overage stipends that would convert this into a wage-adjacent cost; substitute and onboarding costs are still real budget costs even if not wage costs

hypothesis_name: teacher composition effect on observed average wages
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: District or region average teacher salary (e.g., as reported in Massachusetts DESE's Teacher Salaries Report) mixes elementary, middle, secondary, special education, vocational/technical, and bilingual/ESL teachers across the full experience distribution; a district with a higher share of elementary or early-career teachers can show a lower average teacher wage even if its step-and-lane salary schedule is not unusually low relative to peer districts, so naive teacher-vs-police/fire wage comparisons must control for composition rather than comparing raw averages
expected_textual_signals: average teacher salary; FTE by grade level; experience distribution; step and lane placement
likely_source_types: state education agency salary/staffing reports; district HR/personnel data; teacher union contracts
possible_quant_data: average salary by grade band and experience band; step/lane distribution; FTE composition
confounds: composition differences may themselves reflect labor-market or budget conditions (e.g., high turnover skewing a district young), not a pure measurement artifact

hypothesis_name: teacher salary schedule rigidity and shortage-area pay
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: Step-and-lane salary schedules set pay by years of experience and education credits, not by grade or subject, so a shortage in a specific subject (special education, math, science, bilingual/ESL) does not by default raise pay for teachers in that subject; roughly three in four surveyed districts (per NCTQ) offset this with stipends, bonuses, alternative schedules, or extra experience-step credit for hard-to-staff subjects, but about one in four apparently do not, and many such incentives are one-time bonuses or add-ons sitting outside the published base schedule rather than permanent base-wage increases
expected_textual_signals: step and lane; salary schedule; hard-to-fill stipend; shortage-area bonus; extra experience credit; loan forgiveness; differentiated pay; MOU
likely_source_types: district salary schedules; CBAs; NCTQ compensation reports; state teacher-pay policy summaries
possible_quant_data: presence/absence and size of shortage-area stipends; schedule structure (single schedule vs. subject-differentiated); one-time vs. permanent incentive design
confounds: subject-differentiated pay could reflect political/labor-relations bargaining outcomes as much as a market-clearing response to scarcity

hypothesis_name: non-safety wage restraint via institutional/budget translation
non_safety_relevance_or_counterpoint: The police/fire vs. non-safety wage gap could grow not only because safety wages rise faster but because non-safety wage-setting institutions (school committee budgeting under municipal levy limits, absence of an interest-arbitration backstop, uniform multi-year salary schedules) convert comparable labor-market strain into buffering, service adjustment, or budget deferral rather than wage growth; teachers are a first, hard test case because they are credentialed, unionized, and politically salient, so if wage translation is still muted for them, it should be an even sharper contrast for less-salient non-safety units like clerical/admin
expected_textual_signals: budget constraint; levy limit; override; state aid; competitive salary; peer districts; surrounding districts; unable to fund raise
likely_source_types: school committee budgets; municipal finance documents; state aid formulas; teacher/DPW/clerical CBAs for comparison
possible_quant_data: wage growth by occupation class controlling for measured staffing strain (vacancy/applicant data) in the same city and period; frequency of budget override votes tied to compensation
confounds: non-safety units may simply have weaker bargaining power or genuinely smaller supply constraints rather than a distinct institutional-translation effect; fiscal capacity effects (H9) could look similar from the outside
